prompt:
Passage 1
A regional service company recently changed how it handles customer equipment drop-offs. Previously, customers filled out a paper form at the front counter and left the equipment with a receptionist. If the form was incomplete, technicians sometimes had to call the customer before beginning the repair.
The company now uses a digital intake form on a tablet. Customers select the equipment type, describe the problem, provide contact information, and choose whether they want an estimate before repair work begins. The system checks that required fields are completed before the request can be submitted.
Managers report that technicians now receive clearer information at the start of each repair. The front counter staff also spend less time correcting incomplete forms.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: The company stopped repairing customer equipment.
choices.B: Customers now repair their own equipment at the front counter.
choices.C: A digital intake form improved the equipment drop-off process.
choices.D: Technicians no longer communicate with customers.

explanation: The passage focuses on how the digital intake form improved equipment drop-offs by requiring clearer information before repair work begins.

prompt:
Passage 2
NOTICE
Beginning September 3, employees must report damaged extension cords to the tool room before using them again. Damaged cords should be tagged and placed in the marked inspection bin.
This procedure was created after supervisors found several cords with cracked insulation still being used in work areas. The goal is to remove unsafe cords from service until they can be inspected and either repaired or replaced.
Employees should not attempt temporary repairs unless they have been specifically authorized to do so.

What is the purpose of the notice?

choices.A: To announce a new procedure for damaged extension cords
choices.B: To require employees to repair all cords themselves
choices.C: To cancel use of the tool room
choices.D: To introduce a new employee schedule

explanation: The notice announces the procedure for reporting and handling damaged extension cords.

prompt:
Passage 3
Many businesses use delivery windows to manage customer expectations. A delivery window gives customers a range of time when a shipment or service visit is expected to arrive. For example, a customer may be told that a delivery will arrive between 10:00 a.m. and noon.
Delivery windows are useful because exact arrival times can be difficult to promise. Traffic, weather, earlier appointments, loading delays, or route changes may affect a driver’s schedule. A window gives the company some flexibility while still providing customers with planning information.
However, delivery windows must be realistic. If a company regularly misses its promised windows, customers may lose confidence. Companies often review travel times and past delivery data to create more accurate estimates.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Delivery windows are unnecessary when companies have drivers.
choices.B: Delivery windows help manage expectations but must be realistic.
choices.C: Customers should never be told when deliveries may arrive.
choices.D: Exact arrival times are always easy to promise.

explanation: The passage explains that delivery windows help customers plan, but they must be realistic to maintain confidence.

prompt:
Passage 4
Before automatic fire sprinkler systems became common in large buildings, fires often spread more quickly before firefighters arrived. Building occupants had to rely heavily on manual alarms, escape routes, and outside emergency response.
Automatic sprinkler systems changed fire protection by responding directly to heat from a fire. When a sprinkler head is activated, water is released in the affected area. This can slow the spread of fire, reduce damage, and give occupants more time to evacuate.
Sprinkler systems still require regular inspection and maintenance. Pipes, valves, water pressure, and sprinkler heads must be checked to make sure the system is ready to operate when needed.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Fire sprinklers made escape routes unnecessary.
choices.B: Firefighters no longer respond to building fires.
choices.C: Automatic sprinkler systems improved fire protection but require maintenance.
choices.D: Sprinkler systems are used only outdoors.

explanation: The passage explains that sprinkler systems improve fire protection but still require inspections and maintenance.

prompt:
Passage 5
A manufacturing company recently introduced a daily production board near the entrance to each work area. The board shows the day’s production target, current progress, safety reminders, and any equipment or staffing notes that may affect work.
Before the boards were introduced, employees often received updates from supervisors at different times. This sometimes led to confusion when a target changed or when one shift did not receive the same information as another.
Supervisors now update the boards at the beginning of each shift. Employees say the boards make it easier to understand priorities before work begins. Managers also use the boards during short team meetings to review progress and address problems.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: The company removed all supervisor communication.
choices.B: Daily production boards improved communication about work priorities.
choices.C: Employees no longer need safety reminders.
choices.D: Production targets are no longer used.

explanation: The passage focuses on how daily production boards improve communication about targets, progress, safety, and work priorities.

prompt:
Passage 6
Many organizations use inventory labels with both words and symbols. A label might include a product name, number, arrow, color code, or simple image that shows where an item belongs. Combining text with visual cues can help employees locate and return materials more quickly.
Visual cues are especially useful in busy areas where employees may not have time to read long descriptions. For example, a color-coded label on a storage bin can help workers quickly match parts to the correct location.
Still, visual labels must be used consistently. If the same color or symbol means different things in different areas, employees may become confused. A good labeling system uses simple, repeated patterns that workers can learn and apply easily.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Inventory labels are useful only when they use no words.
choices.B: Labels with text and visual cues can improve organization when used consistently.
choices.C: Color codes always create confusion.
choices.D: Storage bins should not include labels.

explanation: The passage explains that labels combining words and symbols can help organization, but they must be used consistently.

prompt:
Passage 7
Many cities operate public tree maintenance programs. City crews may inspect trees near streets, sidewalks, parks, and public buildings. They look for broken limbs, disease, root damage, or branches that interfere with signs, lights, or power lines.
Tree maintenance can improve safety and protect public infrastructure. A weak limb over a sidewalk may fall during a storm, while roots can sometimes damage pavement or underground utilities. Regular inspections help crews identify problems before they become more serious.
Cities must often balance tree preservation with safety concerns. A healthy tree may be trimmed rather than removed, while a severely damaged tree may need to be replaced. Decisions are usually based on condition, location, and risk.

What is the passage mainly about?

choices.A: How cities maintain public trees to manage safety and infrastructure concerns
choices.B: Why cities should remove every tree near a sidewalk
choices.C: How private homeowners sell trees
choices.D: Why public buildings do not need inspections

explanation: The passage explains how cities inspect and maintain public trees while balancing safety and preservation.

prompt:
Passage 8
An apprenticeship readiness program recently added peer study groups to its evening schedule. Program staff noticed that some students studied alone but became discouraged when they could not solve difficult practice questions.
The study groups meet once a week after class. Students compare approaches, explain steps to one another, and review mistakes from practice assignments. An instructor is available nearby but allows students to work through most questions together before stepping in.
Program staff believe the groups help students build confidence and learn from different problem-solving methods. Students also report that the groups make exam preparation feel less isolating.

Why did the program add peer study groups?

choices.A: Some students became discouraged while studying alone.
choices.B: Students no longer needed practice questions.
choices.C: Instructors wanted to cancel evening classes.
choices.D: The program stopped preparing students for exams.

explanation: The program added peer study groups because some students became discouraged when studying difficult practice questions alone.

prompt:
Passage 8
An apprenticeship readiness program recently added peer study groups to its evening schedule. Program staff noticed that some students studied alone but became discouraged when they could not solve difficult practice questions.
The study groups meet once a week after class. Students compare approaches, explain steps to one another, and review mistakes from practice assignments. An instructor is available nearby but allows students to work through most questions together before stepping in.
Program staff believe the groups help students build confidence and learn from different problem-solving methods. Students also report that the groups make exam preparation feel less isolating.

Which conclusion is best supported by the passage?

choices.A: Peer study can help students feel more confident and less isolated.
choices.B: Students learn best when they never explain steps.
choices.C: Practice mistakes should not be reviewed.
choices.D: Study groups prevent students from preparing for exams.

explanation: The passage supports the conclusion that peer study groups can build confidence and make exam preparation feel less isolating.
